topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The chipped ceramic mug felt cold against my palms, mirroring the hollow ache in my chest. Liam laughed, a bright, easy sound that used to instantly warm everything around me. Now, it just felt like an echo in an empty hallway. He was talking about his new job, his eyes sparkling with genuine excitement, and I managed a small, tight smile. It didn't reach my eyes. I watched him, cataloging the familiar contours of his face, searching for the flicker of recognition, the shared history that used to bind us. There was nothing. Just a polite acknowledgment of a familiar voice. A strange, prickly heat began to rise in my neck, and I instinctively pulled my sweater tighter around myself, wishing I could shrink into the worn armchair.

[story 2]

I traced the faded outline of a constellation on the ceiling, the plaster cool beneath my fingertips. The silence in the room was heavy, suffocating. When Mark finally spoke, his tone was warm, familiar, but utterly devoid of the spark I remembered. "That was a good trip," he said, shifting his weight. I nodded, my throat suddenly dry. I tried to keep my expression neutral, but a tremor ran through my hands, making the pencil I held slip slightly. *He doesn't remember,* the thought echoed, sharp and undeniable. I felt a sudden pressure behind my eyes, a strange blurring of the edges of the room. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to anchor myself, but the ground felt unsteady beneath my feet.

[story 3]

It happened over lukewarm coffee at the usual corner booth. Sarah recounted an anecdote about a summer vacation, detailing every silly inside joke we’d ever shared. I listened, my posture rigid, my gaze fixed on the swirling foam in my cup. When she paused, expecting a knowing glance, there was only blankness. A slow, painful deflation washed over me, like a punctured balloon. I forced a small, almost brittle sound of agreement. My jaw tightened, and I focused intently on the pattern of the condensation dripping down the side of the glass. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring, yet utterly paralyzed. I just wanted the moment to dissolve, to vanish into the background noise of the cafe.
